---
title: "Other Civic Data Sources"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    embed-resources: true
---

Civic data access refers to the practice of providing the public with free, transparent, and machine-readable information generated by government entities, non-profits, and civic institutions. It functions as a pillar of modern open democracy by allowing citizens, researchers, and developers to monitor policies, build public-interest apps, and track community life.🌐 

Key Portals and Platforms

Several major software networks and public portals serve as central hubs for civic data access:

CivicData: 

A free, open-source platform built on CKAN technology by Accela. It aggregates up-to-date regional data from dozens of local and state government agencies into a single API-accessible location.

Data.gov: 

The central clearinghouse for the United States federal government's open data, offering hundreds of thousands of datasets.

State Open Data Portals: 

Most states manage localized repositories, such as the 

Texas Open Data Portal, 

to host official agency statistics, financial audits, and public records.🛠️ Developer and API ToolsFor software engineers and civic hackers building public-interest tech, direct data feeds are highly accessible:


Google Civic Information API: 

Maps US residential addresses to specific elected officials across federal, state, and local districts. It provides polling places, candidate data, and election official contact sheets.


U.S. Vote Foundation API:

Offers specialized, custom widgets and data licensing options tailored for tracking domestic absentee and vote-by-mail ballot processes.🏛️

Types of Information Available

Publicly accessible civic datasets generally cover four broad categories:


Elections and Representation: 

Polling locations, registration dates, candidate rosters, and political boundary maps.

Legislation and Policy: 

State or federal bill introductions, committee hearing testimonies, and voting histories via tools like Plural Policy.


Municipal Infrastructure:

Real-time traffic, code violations, public transport schedules, building permits, and localized zoning structures.


Community Safety and Health:

Regional crime statistics, emergency health updates, and local environmental metrics.
